name: Royal Mausoleum (Mauna 'Ala)
tagline: The final resting place of Hawaiian royalty — six kings, six queens, and other ali'i in a quiet Nu'uanu chapel surrounded by sacred ground.
parking: Small parking area at the entrance on Nu'uanu Ave
hours: Mon-Fri 8am-4pm; closed weekends and state holidays
bestTime: Weekday mornings — you'll likely have the grounds to yourself
talkStory: Mauna 'Ala ('fragrant mountain') was established in 1865 by Kamehameha V after the original royal tomb at Iolani Palace ran out of space. The Gothic Revival chapel holds the remains of six kings, six queens, and dozens of high-ranking ali'i from the Kamehameha and Kalakaua dynasties. A single caretaker from the State of Hawaii tends the grounds — a position that has existed continuously since the Kingdom era. The site is sovereign Hawaiian territory by law, one of the few places where the Hawaiian flag still flies without an American flag beside it.
